response_text:
# JavaScript's Threading Model

## The Core Reality

**JavaScript is single-threaded.** It does not have a built-in multi-threading model in the traditional sense. There is one main thread, one call stack, and one thing executing at a time.

```javascript
// This all runs on ONE thread, sequentially
console.log("first");
console.log("second");  // "second" CANNOT run until "first" finishes
console.log("third");
```

---

## How JavaScript Handles Concurrency

JavaScript achieves *concurrency* (not parallelism) through the **Event Loop**.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   Call Stack                     │
│  [ main() → setTimeout cb → fetch cb → ... ]    │
└─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
                      │ pops tasks when empty
┌─────────────────────▼───────────────────────────┐
│                  Event Loop                      │
│   Checks: "Is the call stack empty?"            │
│   If yes: move next task from queue to stack    │
└──────────┬──────────────────────┬───────────────┘
           │                      │
┌──────────▼──────────┐  ┌───────▼───────────────┐
│   Microtask Queue   │  │   Macrotask Queue      │
│  (Promises, queueM  │  │  (setTimeout,          │
│   icrotask)         │  │   setInterval, I/O)    │
│  Higher priority    │  │   Lower priority       │
└─────────────────────┘  └───────────────────────┘
           ▲                      ▲
           └──────────┬───────────┘
                      │
┌─────────────────────▼───────────────────────────┐
│              Web APIs / Node APIs                │
│   (fetch, setTimeout, fs.readFile, etc.)        │
│   These run OUTSIDE the JS thread               │
└─────────────
